title: Supreme Court Ponders GST ITC's Toughest Knot: A Deep Dive into the Challenge to Section 16(2)(c)
content:
# Supreme Court Ponders GST ITC's Toughest Knot: A Deep Dive into the Challenge to Section 16(2)(c)

As tax professionals, we often find ourselves navigating the intricate labyrinth of GST law, and perhaps no provision has generated more anxiety and litigation than **Section 16(2)(c) of the CGST Act, 2017**. This clause, which makes Input Tax Credit (ITC) dependent on the actual payment of tax by the supplier to the government, has been a constant source of grief for genuine businesses. The good news? The Supreme Court has formally taken cognizance and issued notice in a constitutional challenge to this very provision, signaling a potential turning point for taxpayer rights. This is a big deal, *yaar*, and something we all need to understand deeply.

## The ITC Conundrum: When Compliance Becomes a Catch-22

Imagine this: You’ve purchased goods or services, paid the full invoice value including GST, received the supplies, and duly filed your returns. All boxes ticked from your end, *ekdum sahi*. Yet, you are denied ITC because your supplier, perhaps several layers up the chain, failed to deposit the tax with the government. This is the harsh reality that **Section 16(2)(c)** often imposes. The provision states that a registered person is not entitled to ITC unless "the tax charged in respect of such supply has been actually paid to the Government, either in cash or through utilisation of input tax credit admissible in respect of the said supply".

On the face of it, the intent is clear: prevent revenue leakage and tackle fraudulent transactions. But the practical implication? It thrusts an impossible burden (*lex non cogit ad impossibilia* – the law does not compel a man to do that which he cannot possibly perform) upon the recipient. How is a buyer supposed to audit the backend compliance of their supplier, let alone an upstream supplier? This **"Catch-22" situation** penalizes the honest taxpayer for the default of a third party over whom they have no control.

## The Road to the Apex Court: M/s Prime Metals Rings the Bell

This contentious issue has seen various High Courts across the country deliver divergent judgments. While some High Courts have upheld the constitutional validity of Section 16(2)(c), others have tried to read it down or provide relief to bona fide purchasers. The stage was thus set for the Supreme Court to provide a definitive stance.

On May 22, 2026, the Hon'ble Supreme Court, comprising Justice K.V. Viswanathan and Justice Vijay Bishnoi, took up the **Special Leave Petition (C) No. 18577/2026** filed by **M/s Prime Metals, Alwar (Rajasthan)**. The petitioner had initially approached the Rajasthan High Court, challenging a Show Cause Notice and an adjudication order, and crucially, the constitutional validity of Section 16(2)(c). However, the High Court dismissed the writ petition, relegating the assessee to the alternative remedy of statutory appeal.

The Supreme Court, however, acknowledged the petitioner's argument that a constitutional challenge to a statutory provision, being a question of general public importance, cannot be adequately adjudicated through an alternative remedy of appeal. Consequently, the Apex Court issued notice, signaling its intent to delve into the constitutional validity and interpretation of Section 16(2)(c). The matter was subsequently listed for further consideration on May 29, 2026.

## The Core Constitutional Arguments: Why 16(2)(c) is Under Scrutiny

The challenge to Section 16(2)(c) primarily hinges on several fundamental constitutional principles:

*   **Violation of Article 14 (Equality Before Law):** Petitioners argue that the provision is manifestly arbitrary as it fails to differentiate between a bona fide purchaser who has diligently complied with all obligations and a fraudulent one. By treating both on the same footing and denying ITC, it violates the principle of equality. An innocent buyer, having paid their share of tax, should not be punished for someone else's misdeeds.
*   **Violation of Article 19(1)(g) (Right to Carry on Trade or Profession):** Denying ITC to a genuine recipient due to supplier default imposes an unreasonable restriction on the right to carry on trade. It effectively forces businesses to operate with a higher cost burden, impacting their working capital and competitiveness.
*   **Doctrine of Impossibility (*Lex Non Cogit Ad Impossibilia*):** This is a cornerstone argument. How can the law expect a buyer to ensure the supplier deposits tax? There is no statutory or technological mechanism for the recipient to verify or control the supplier’s tax payment post-transaction. Once the buyer pays the consideration including tax, their role (*functus officio*) is complete.
*   **Double Taxation and Unjust Enrichment:** If ITC is denied, the government effectively collects tax twice – once from the supplier (which they should recover if unpaid) and again from the recipient by disallowing their credit. This leads to the unjust enrichment of the State, running contrary to the very spirit of GST, which aims to eliminate the cascading effect of taxes.
*   **ITC as a Vested Right:** While some High Courts view ITC as a mere concession, the argument is that once all conditions within the buyer's control are met, it becomes a vested right. Previous Supreme Court pronouncements under the VAT regime (like the *Arise India Ltd. case*) have protected bona fide purchasers, and similar principles are being sought for GST.

## The Other Side of the Coin: Revenue's Stance and Supporting Judgments

It's important to understand the arguments in favor of Section 16(2)(c) as well. The revenue authorities and several High Courts have upheld its validity on grounds such as:

*   **Statutory Concession, Not Absolute Right:** They contend that ITC is a "concession" or "entitlement" provided under the statute, subject to all prescribed conditions and restrictions. If a condition (like tax payment by the supplier) is not met, the credit simply doesn't accrue.
*   **Safeguarding Revenue:** The primary objective of the provision is to prevent tax evasion and ensure that the government actually receives the tax collected. Without it, the GST framework, especially for inter-state transactions, could suffer massive revenue losses.
*   **Integrated GST Framework:** Unlike the erstwhile VAT regime, GST operates on an integrated credit chain. Provisions like **Section 41** (which has seen amendments related to provisional ITC) and **Section 155** (placing the burden of proof on the claimant) are cited to support the strict conditions for ITC availment.
*   **Self-Assessment Mechanism:** The GST system relies on self-assessment, and the conditions in Section 16(2)(c) are seen as integral to this system, requiring diligence from taxpayers to ensure their entire supply chain is compliant.

Recent judgments, such as the Gujarat High Court in *Maruti Enterprise v. Union of India* (May 1, 2026), have upheld the validity of Section 16(2)(c), emphasizing that ITC is intrinsically linked to actual tax payment and is not a vested right. However, even this judgment urged the government to implement technology-driven mechanisms to protect genuine purchasers.

## What Lies Ahead: *Kaisa Mod Lega Yeh Mamla*?

The Supreme Court entertaining this challenge is a significant development. It means the Apex Court recognizes the fundamental legal and constitutional questions at play, moving beyond mere procedural issues. While the Supreme Court has only issued notice and no final adjudication on merits has been made, the very fact that it has taken cognizance of the "impossibility of performance" argument under the GST framework is a huge ray of hope for the business community.

This case has the potential to redefine the rights of bona fide purchasers under GST. Will the Supreme Court read down Section 16(2)(c) to protect innocent buyers, or will it uphold its strict interpretation, perhaps with directions for legislative or administrative safeguards? The outcome will have far-reaching implications, impacting businesses, compliance mechanisms, and the very philosophy of ITC under GST.

Till then, as prudent professionals, we must continue to advise our clients to exercise extreme due diligence when dealing with suppliers. Verify their compliance, ensure timely payments, and maintain robust documentation – *kyunki abhi toh picture baaki hai, mere dost*. This is a space we will be watching closely, and you should too.

## Read the Official Document
* [Supreme Court of India](https://main.sci.gov.in)

*Published on: 31 May 2026*
